network.overall_strategy: This network models asparagine as a transient, late-forming prebiotic amino acid assembled through a multi-environment system rather than a single continuous setting. Hydrothermal environments generate core carbon skeletons and protometabolic precursors such as pyruvate and oxaloacetate; surface cyanosulfidic, Strecker, and discharge chemistries generate cyanoacetylene, cyanoacetaldehyde, aminonitriles, and direct asparagine; biochemical/prebiotic settings provide limited amidation, hydrolysis control, and kinetic trapping into peptides. The network is literature-guided and explicitly reflects the major constraint that asparagine is easier to form than to preserve because deamidation to aspartate is thermodynamically favored.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to pyruvate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed CO2 fixation and sequential reduction/carboxylation generate pyruvate as a low-yield C3 product in iron-sulfur hydrothermal systems.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 under hydrothermal iron-sulfur catalysis is supported by vent-simulation studies showing formate, acetate, and pyruvate formation on greigite/magnetite under alkaline conditions (Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Protometabolic carboxylation of pyruvate to oxaloacetate

conditions.other: anoxic protometabolic network; oxaloacetate recognized as unstable and transient

reactions.mechanism: Carboxylative network chemistry extends the C3 ketoacid scaffold to the C4 alpha-ketoacid oxaloacetate in an iron-promoted protometabolic setting.
reactions.reasoning: Protometabolic iron-promoted networks connecting pyruvate, glyoxylate, and Krebs-like intermediates support transient oxaloacetate accessibility, though instability is a major bottleneck (Muchowska et al., 2019; Harrison et al., 2023). This connection is included as a transient hydrothermal route required by the literature-guided aspartate network.

reactions.name: Transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.other: protometabolic amination/transamination regime; oxaloacetate instability competes via decarboxylation

reactions.mechanism: Amination/transamination of the alpha-ketoacid oxaloacetate yields aspartate in iron-promoted protometabolic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Aspartate formation from oxaloacetate is literature-supported through prebiotic transamination and protometabolic networks, although oxaloacetate decarboxylation is a major competing process (Harrison et al., 2023; Muchowska et al., 2019; Springsteen et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface generation of cyanoacetylene from HCN feedstock

conditions.catalyst: Cu photoredox system associated with sulfide-rich UV-exposed settings
conditions.other: 35°C, anoxic, phosphate-buffered, UV irradiation; cyanosulfidic conditions

reactions.mechanism: HCN enters UV-driven cyanosulfidic photochemistry, producing cyanoacetylene through oxidative cross-coupling chemistry in sulfide-rich surface pools.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) showed cyanosulfidic chemistry from HCN and H2S under UV with Cu photoredox cycling can generate cyanoacetylene. Mineral sulfides and UV-active surfaces are used here to place the reaction within the required surface mineral framework while retaining the literature conditions (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of cyanoacetylene to cyanoacetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene undergoes hydration/hydrolysis to cyanoacetaldehyde, generating the key Strecker-compatible beta-cyano aldehyde intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanoacetaldehyde as a hydrolysis product of cyanoacetylene is explicitly identified in the Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel route and in the research summary as a key branching intermediate toward asparagine/aspartate (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966). Mineral assignment is connective but the core transformation is literature-based.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic conversion of cyanoacetylene to maleonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in phosphate buffer
conditions.other: 35°C, UV irradiation, alkaline phosphate buffer, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Within the cyanosulfidic network, cyanoacetylene reacts in the presence of excess HCN and NH3 under UV-driven redox cycling to form dinitrile intermediates including maleonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly identified maleonitrile as an intermediate on the route from cyanoacetylene toward alpha-aminonitrile compound 49, precursor to asparagine/aspartate.

reactions.name: Formation of alpha-aminonitrile precursor from cyanoacetaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline aqueous setting; selective partial hydrolysis remains challenging

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetaldehyde undergoes Strecker-type addition of ammonia and HCN to generate the four-carbon alpha-aminonitrile precursor to asparagine and aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: The research summary identifies cyanoacetaldehyde + NH3 + HCN as the Strecker route to a dinitrile/aminonitrile intermediate leading to asparagine upon partial hydrolysis and aspartate upon full hydrolysis (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966).

reactions.name: Formation of alpha-aminonitrile precursor from maleonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Maleonitrile is aminated within the cyanosulfidic network to produce the alpha-aminonitrile identified by Patel et al. as compound 49.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly report alpha-aminonitrile compound 49 as the precursor to asparagine and aspartate in their cyanosulfidic system.

reactions.name: Partial hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitrile to asparagine

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions favoring incomplete nitrile hydrolysis; kinetically fragile product

reactions.mechanism: Selective partial hydrolysis converts one nitrile functionality of the C4 aminonitrile into a side-chain amide while retaining the amino acid framework, giving asparagine.
reactions.reasoning: Partial hydrolysis of the alpha-aminonitrile precursor to asparagine is the key final step proposed for both the cyanoacetylene and cyanosulfidic routes, though selective stopping at the amide is a major unresolved challenge (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Patel et al., 2015; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Full hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitrile to aspartate

conditions.other: more extensive hydrolysis than rxn_009; thermodynamically favored relative to amide retention

reactions.mechanism: Further hydrolysis of the nitrile-bearing precursor proceeds past the amide stage to the dicarboxylate amino acid aspartate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature consistently identifies aspartate as the full-hydrolysis product corresponding to the same aminonitrile precursors that can also yield asparagine by partial hydrolysis (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct cyanoacetylene aqueous synthesis of asparagine

conditions.other: simple inorganic aqueous solution; reducing-atmosphere-derived cyanoacetylene source

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene reacts in aqueous inorganic solution through cyanoacetaldehyde and aminonitrile intermediates to furnish both asparagine and aspartate directly within the same chemistry set.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) experimentally demonstrated that cyanoacetylene reacts with simple inorganic substances in aqueous solution to yield aspartic acid, asparagine, and cytosine.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge direct formation of asparagine from simple gases

conditions.other: reducing discharge chemistry; product only visible when analyzed without acid hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through simple gaseous feedstocks generates complex nitrile-rich organics that include free asparagine among amino acid products.
reactions.reasoning: Jiang et al. (2014) detected asparagine directly, and as the highest-abundance amino acid, in spark discharge products using LC-MS/MS without acid hydrolysis; this reaction is represented as a direct surface-discharge route because the exact detailed microsequence was not resolved in the report.

reactions.name: Contact glow discharge conversion of alanine and formamide to asparagine

conditions.other: alanine 100 mM, formamide 200 mM, contact glow discharge electrolysis; reported 3.0% yield

reactions.mechanism: Plasma-driven activation of alanine and formamide generates higher amino acids including asparagine through oxidative/reductive radical chemistry in aqueous formamide-containing solution.
reactions.reasoning: Munegumi (2014) reported 3.0% asparagine yield from alanine and formamide under contact glow discharge electrolysis, simulating volcanic lightning chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed reductive amination of the alpha-ketoacid pyruvate yields alanine, linking hydrothermal carbon fixation chemistry to later discharge-driven asparagine formation.
reactions.reasoning: FeS/FeS2-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoacids to amino acids is supported by hydrothermal iron-sulfur chemistry, and pyruvate-to-alanine conversion is a canonical example (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2003; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Speculative prebiotic amidation of aspartate to asparagine via activating agents

conditions.other: dry-phase or low-water activity amidation using candidate activating agents cyanamide, DAP, COS, or urea; no direct demonstration for asparagine

reactions.mechanism: Activated condensation of aspartate side-chain carboxylate with ammonia is proposed to yield the side-chain amide of asparagine under dehydrating or condensing conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The research report identifies amidation of aspartate as the principal unsolved bottleneck and names diamidophosphate, cyanamide, carbonyl sulfide, and urea as candidate prebiotic activating agents, but notes that none have been specifically demonstrated for asparagine formation; this reaction is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because it is necessary to connect the documented aspartate-rich network to the target (Osumah & Krishnamurthy, 2021; Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Leman et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Deamidation of asparagine to aspartate

conditions.other: thermodynamically favored hydrolysis; rapid at elevated temperature

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of the side-chain amide converts asparagine to aspartate and ammonia, draining asparagine pools.
reactions.reasoning: Asparagine hydrolysis to aspartate is exothermic and strongly favored; its instability is central to the literature and explains why asparagine is difficult to accumulate prebiotically (Kishore, Tewari & Goldberg, 2000; Bada, Miller & Zhao, 1995; Robinson & Robinson, 2001).

reactions.name: Kinetic trapping of asparagine into peptide products

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling and condensation-prone conditions; rapid incorporation proposed as escape from deamidation

reactions.mechanism: Transient asparagine is incorporated into peptide-like products, reducing exposure of the free amino acid pool to hydrolytic destruction.
reactions.reasoning: The research report highlights kinetic trapping and notes that asparagine-containing dipeptides polymerize faster than asparagine alone, suggesting peptide incorporation as a plausible escape route from deamidation (Munegumi & Tanikawa, 2017).

pathways.name: Sanchez-Orgel direct cyanoacetylene pathway
pathways.description: Direct aqueous cyanoacetylene chemistry yields asparagine in a surface setting.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetylene hydrolysis then Strecker-type aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Cyanoacetylene is hydrolyzed to cyanoacetaldehyde, which enters Strecker chemistry to an aminonitrile that partially hydrolyzes to asparagine.

pathways.name: Patel cyanosulfidic route through maleonitrile
pathways.description: HCN/H2S/UV cyanosulfidic chemistry forms cyanoacetylene, maleonitrile, then the alpha-aminonitrile precursor that partially hydrolyzes to asparagine.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetaldehyde branch from cyanoacetylene aqueous chemistry
pathways.description: The Sanchez route is expanded explicitly through the cyanoacetaldehyde branch and selective partial hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Direct spark discharge pathway
pathways.description: Electrical discharge chemistry from simple feedstocks generates asparagine directly, detectable only without acid hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolism to aspartate then speculative amidation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation produces pyruvate, carboxylation gives oxaloacetate, transamination gives aspartate, and a speculative prebiotic amidation yields asparagine.

pathways.name: Aminonitrile full hydrolysis to aspartate then amidation
pathways.description: The cyanoacetaldehyde Strecker branch first yields aspartate by full hydrolysis, followed by speculative amidation to asparagine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminonitrile full hydrolysis to aspartate then amidation
pathways.description: The Patel maleonitrile branch converges on the same alpha-aminonitrile, which fully hydrolyzes to aspartate and is then amidated speculatively.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to alanine then glow-discharge asparagine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated to alanine, which in formamide under contact glow discharge yields asparagine.

pathways.name: Direct asparagine formation followed by kinetic trapping
pathways.description: Asparagine formed directly by discharge or cyanoacetylene chemistry is rapidly incorporated into peptide-like material to escape deamidation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature alignment with the classic Sanchez-Ferris-Orgel cyanoacetylene route. Direct aqueous conversion of cyanoacetylene-derived chemistry to asparagine is one of the best-supported prebiotic entries to the target. Main caveat is dependence on cyanoacetylene availability under realistically weakly reducing atmospheres.
suggestions: Explicitly specify the mineral/agent basis for cyanoacetylene generation and aqueous reaction conditions.
Note atmospheric dependence and likely need for localized volcanic/reducing environments.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway matches the literature-supported cyanoacetaldehyde/Strecker-type logic toward a C4 aminonitrile precursor. The main weakness is the selective partial hydrolysis step from aminonitrile to asparagine, which is chemically plausible but difficult to control prebiotically and is a major challenge emphasized in the literature.
suggestions: Add stronger justification for kinetic control or limited-water conditions enabling partial rather than full hydrolysis.
Clarify whether the aminonitrile is explicitly the Patel/Sanchez precursor or a more generic Strecker intermediate.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good alignment with Patel et al. cyanosulfidic chemistry: cyanoacetylene to maleonitrile to alpha-aminonitrile precursor is literature grounded. Again the bottleneck is selective partial hydrolysis to the amide instead of full hydrolysis to aspartate. This is one of the best multi-step literature-based routes in the network.
suggestions: Specify UV, phosphate-buffered, anoxic, approximately 35 C conditions more explicitly.
Clarify if Cu photoredox is assumed; if so, it should be represented among agents even if mineral support is also given.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The branch is chemically sensible up to cyanoacetaldehyde and aminonitrile formation, but the endpoint appears somewhat redundant or internally inconsistent if it returns to direct cyanoacetylene aqueous synthesis rather than continuing via the aminonitrile intermediate. Pathway naming suggests one route while the listed chemistry overlaps two distinct literature mechanisms.
suggestions: Separate the direct Sanchez route from the cyanoacetaldehyde-Strecker route more cleanly.
Ensure the reaction sequence does not double-count parallel alternatives as a single linear pathway.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well aligned with Jiang et al. in the sense that direct asparagine formation in spark discharge products is experimentally reported when acid hydrolysis is avoided. The limitation is that this is analytically supported product detection rather than a mechanistically well-resolved pathway, and the surface-environment labeling is somewhat stretched because spark discharge is more atmospheric/plume chemistry than mineral-mediated surface chemistry.
suggestions: Represent this as atmospheric discharge feeding surface collection rather than purely surface chemistry.
Include the analytical caveat that non-hydrolytic detection is essential.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest major route. Hydrothermal pyruvate generation is literature-supported, but hydrothermal oxaloacetate formation and especially direct transamination with NH3 are not well supported as written. The amidation of aspartate to asparagine using DAP/cyanamide/COS/urea is explicitly speculative and currently unsupported experimentally for this substrate. The pathway is conceptually useful as a multi-environment hypothesis but not convincing as a demonstrated synthesis route.
suggestions: Replace direct NH3 transamination with literature-supported transamination chemistry involving pyridoxamine, hydroxylamine/Fe0, or Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry.
Downgrade amidation from pathway core to hypothetical branch unless direct evidence is added.
Account for oxaloacetate instability and decarboxylation back to pyruvate.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The first four steps are coherent and literature grounded, especially the full hydrolysis of aminonitrile to aspartate. However, the final amidation remains the unresolved bottleneck in prebiotic asparagine chemistry. As a result this pathway is strong to aspartate but weak to asparagine.
suggestions: Reframe the pathway as a robust route to aspartate with hypothetical extension to asparagine.
If retained, include stronger discussion of why activation agents might transiently produce low Asn yields despite unfavorable thermodynamics.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Like P7, this is convincing through the aspartate stage and well anchored in Patel-style chemistry. It fails mainly at the final amidation step, which the literature specifically identifies as unsolved. Good as a precursor pathway, not yet as a validated asparagine pathway.
suggestions: Present aspartate as the validated product and asparagine amidation as a research gap.
Consider coupling amidation claims to rapid trapping into peptides if the route is preserved.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is a reasonable hybrid path: hydrothermal pyruvate to alanine is plausible, and alanine plus formamide under contact glow discharge has direct literature support from Munegumi. The main concern is that the final discharge chemistry is not really 'biochemical' but a distinct energy-driven geophysical environment. Still, as a target-achieving route it is credible.
suggestions: Reclassify the final step as discharge/plasma surface-atmosphere chemistry rather than biochemical.
Clarify whether alanine concentration and formamide-rich conditions are realistic in the proposed setting.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The idea of direct asparagine formation followed by kinetic trapping into peptides is strongly motivated by the literature, which highlights trapping as a possible solution to instability. However, the peptide-trapping reaction is underspecified and chemically weaker than the direct formation step. Without explicit activation chemistry, the trapping step is only moderately plausible.
suggestions: Specify a condensation-promoting context such as COS, wet-dry cycling, or mineral-assisted peptide formation.
Indicate whether the peptide product is meant as an illustrative sink rather than a demonstrated specific product.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong, literature-aware network that captures the central truth about prebiotic asparagine: synthesis is possible by several routes, but persistence is poor because deamidation to aspartate is favored. Topologically, the network is well connected, has adequate node counts, identifies sensible hubs, and includes multiple convergent routes to the target and to key intermediates such as cyanoacetylene, aminonitrile precursor, pyruvate, and aspartate. Environmental interconnection is also good in concept, especially the hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical flow. The network aligns well with major literature routes: Sanchez-Orgel cyanoacetylene chemistry, Patel cyanosulfidic chemistry, Jiang spark discharge detection, and Munegumi glow discharge conversion. It also appropriately includes the destructive deamidation sink and a kinetic trapping concept. The principal weaknesses are chemical overreach in the hydrothermal protometabolic branch and in the speculative amidation of aspartate. Oxaloacetate chemistry is overstated relative to its instability, direct NH3 transamination is not well justified, and no prebiotic amidation of aspartate to asparagine has been experimentally demonstrated despite inclusion of multiple activating agents. Another issue is schema-level environmental categorization: some discharge/plasma reactions are labeled as Surface or Biochemical when they are better treated as atmospheric or geoelectrochemical contexts. Finally, because the source summary omits detailed per-reaction agents/conditions/citations, the network cannot fully satisfy the strict mineral-agent and literature-citation requirements even if the underlying chemistry is mostly consistent. Overall, this is a good but not approval-grade network under the >=0.9 threshold: excellent in literature awareness and network design, but held back by one major unresolved bottleneck and a few overconfident mechanistic assignments.
